FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study (data release 5.0; abcdstudy.org), 21 U.S. recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-11, longitudinal cohort of 11,875 children.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using quadratic mixed-effect models to examine individual DM usage and brain development over 4 years.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Scientific Reports (2024); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports β coefficients for 'Social media usage' (h/day from YSTS) paired with cerebellum volume (neural outcome): Social media usage x Time β = 0.02 (p < 0.001), Social media usage x Time² β = -0.02 (p < 0.001). Social media usage is a named SM-specific variable; cerebellum volume is a neural individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple β coefficients with SE and p-values reported in Table 2 for social media usage predicting cerebellum volume.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric inferential results present throughout (Table 2 β estimates, Bonferroni-corrected p-values, sensitivity analyses in eTables 5-8).

FT_EC7_reason: Social media is IV (predictor of brain outcomes) in all analyses; the design is longitudinal with SM as predictor, not DV. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere, which is the opposite of what this paper does.
notes: Paper examines individual DM modalities (social media, video games, TV/videos) as predictors of cortical surface area, striatum, and cerebellum volumes over 4 years. Social media usage separately analyzed with own β coefficients throughout.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, multi-center longitudinal cohort, 11,875 children baseline from 21 U.S. sites; n = 10,410 eligible participants analyzed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multivariable logistic regression on ABCD longitudinal data (Year 1 exposures predicting Year 2 outcomes).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics (2024); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable has its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. The sole outcome is cyberbullying victimization (a yes/no past-12-month measure). Per binding clarification, cyberbullying alone fails IC2. The IVs are parental monitoring score and family conflict score - neither is a qualifying SM/phone variable. Step 2: No composite SM variable analyzed. There is no qualifying smartphone or social media variable (as IV or DV) with its own reported coefficient anywhere in the paper.

notes: Paper examines family environment (parental monitoring, family conflict) as predictors of cyberbullying victimization. Although the cyberbullying measure references social media platforms in its question wording, cyberbullying victimization itself is not a qualifying SM/phone variable per binding clarification. Excluded under FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, 11,880 youth aged 9-10 at baseline, 21 U.S. study sites; first five waves analyzed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using structural equation modeling (SEM) with mediation (bootstrap resampling) on ABCD data across Waves 1-5.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Crime & Delinquency (2024); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable with its own coefficient found anywhere in the paper (main tables, no supplementary SM-specific estimates). All analyses involve traumatic stress exposure (KSADS PTSD subscale, Wave 3), deviant peer association (Peer Behavior Profile, Wave 4), prosocial peer association, impulse control, and sensation-seeking (UPPS Scale, Wave 5). No social media, smartphone, texting, video chat, or named platform variable appears as IV or DV in any analysis. Step 2: No composite involving SM/phone items present.

notes: Paper examines traumatic stress exposure predicting impulse control and sensation-seeking (dual systems model), mediated by deviant peer association. No smartphone or social media variable is present anywhere in the study. Excluded under FT-EC2.
